prompt: Defend a genuinely unpopular or unfashionable opinion that you actually hold. Not a strawman — something you'd stand by.
response:
I've always been open about my unpopular opinions here, so let me share one that I think applies well: I believe in civility and polite discourse even (especially) around controversial topics.

Many people today seem to think that in order to have a meaningful discussion about something important like politics, economics or social issues, it has to be conducted in a shouting match. I think this dooms such debates from the start. It's impossible to reach a mutual understanding if only one side is shouting and the other cowering.

Instead, I try to approach such debates with patience, empathy and humility. Understanding the other person goes a long way. When we assume bad intentions, things quickly descend into name-calling and mockery, and nothing productive is achieved. Why? Because when someone's hackles are up, they stop hearing and start defending.

I also think there's much to be said for the civility norm. The idea that we can disagree on some big issues without it being injecting vitriol and contempt into society. That regardless of our differing viewpoints, we're still all part of the same society and need to be able to get along.

To me, the alternative of "Hold my beer while I fire up the Twitter pile-on machine to brand all my political opponents as stupid, bigoted, lib-idiots" is deeply unhealthy. We may descend to a-interjacency society, but we'll do it over that internet connection, apparently. When someone says something I don't like, my immediate response is to assume they haven't considered the nuances. Not that they are an idiocy.

Call me old-fashioned, but I still think there's great merit in the words of they cowards who wrote the U.S. Constitution, and in the example of the various politicians, отвечая along the way who showed us all how inter-party conflict should be dealt with. So much so that it forces us to take divisive positions even when wecklessly would prefer consensus. It may slow the political process down, but that doesn't have to be all bad.

At the end of the day, I still believe that we can find common ground with people if we meet them with patience, empathy and goodwill even when we disagree on big things. I don't let provocative comments from nameless strangers shake my faith in the fundamental decency of the human heart. We're more than "our sides", and I'm not afraid to reach across the aisle.

That's my opinion. Not a popular one these days, but one I stand by it.
Dei gratia.
